questions.text: In Mark 1, what were Simon and Andrew doing when Jesus called them?
questions.explanation: Mark says they were casting a net into the sea; they were fishermen.

questions.text: True or False: In Mark 1, Jesus was baptized by John.
questions.explanation: Mark records Jesus being baptized by John in the Jordan.

questions.text: In Mark 1, where did Jesus teach and cast out an unclean spirit?
questions.explanation: Mark places this event in the synagogue at Capernaum.

questions.text: In Mark 1, when did people bring the sick to Jesus at Simon’s house?
options: At sunrise with coffee and bagels
questions.explanation: Mark says it was at evening, when the sun had set.

questions.text: True or False: In Mark 1, Jesus healed Simon’s mother-in-law of a fever.
questions.explanation: Jesus took her by the hand and the fever left her.

questions.text: In Mark 1, what did Jesus say to the man with leprosy before healing him?
options: Your faith has made you well
questions.explanation: Jesus said, “I am willing. Be made clean.”

questions.text: In Mark 1, where did Jesus go early in the morning to pray?
questions.explanation: Mark says Jesus went out to a solitary place and prayed.

questions.text: True or False: After cleansing the man with leprosy, Jesus told him to tell everyone immediately.
questions.explanation: Jesus told him to say nothing and to show himself to the priest.

questions.text: In Mark 2, how was the paralyzed man brought to Jesus?
questions.explanation: They opened the roof above Jesus and lowered the mat.

questions.text: In Mark 2, what did Jesus say first to the paralyzed man?
questions.explanation: Jesus said, “Son, your sins are forgiven.”

questions.text: In Mark 2, who did Jesus call while he was sitting at the tax booth?
questions.explanation: Mark says Jesus called Levi and he followed him.

questions.text: True or False: In Mark 2, Jesus said, “Those who are well have no need of a physician.”
questions.explanation: Jesus used this saying while speaking about calling sinners.

questions.text: In Mark 2, what illustration did Jesus use to explain why his disciples did not fast?
questions.explanation: Jesus compared it to wedding guests with the bridegroom.

questions.text: In Mark 2, what were the disciples doing on the Sabbath that led to questions?
questions.explanation: They began to pluck heads of grain as they went.

questions.text: True or False: In Mark 2, Jesus said, “The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath.”
questions.explanation: This saying is recorded in Mark’s Sabbath discussion.

questions.text: In Mark 3, what did Jesus tell the man with the withered hand to do?
questions.explanation: Jesus told him, “Stretch out your hand,” and it was restored.

questions.text: In Mark 3, who went out and plotted with the Herodians against Jesus?
questions.explanation: Mark says the Pharisees went out and took counsel with the Herodians.

questions.text: In Mark 3, how many did Jesus appoint to be with him and to be sent out?
questions.explanation: Mark says Jesus appointed twelve.

questions.text: True or False: In Mark 3, Jesus warned about blasphemy against the Holy Spirit.
questions.explanation: Mark records Jesus warning about an eternal sin in this context.

questions.text: In Mark 3, who did Jesus say are his brother and sister and mother?
options: Those who do the will of God
questions.explanation: Jesus said whoever does the will of God is his family.

questions.text: In the Protestant Bible, how many books are there total?
questions.explanation: Most Protestant Bibles use a 66-book canon (39 Old Testament books and 27 New Testament books). This is a historical fact about the Protestant canon; Scripture itself does not give a numbered list of the books.
questions.learnMore: Different Christian traditions (for example, Catholic and Orthodox) recognize different numbers of books. This app follows the 66-book Protestant canon.

questions.text: In the Protestant Bible, how many books are in the Old Testament?
questions.explanation: This app uses the 66-book Protestant canon: 39 OT books and 27 NT books.

questions.text: In the Protestant Bible, how many books are in the New Testament?
questions.explanation: This app uses the 66-book Protestant canon: 27 NT books.

questions.text: According to Genesis, who were the first two people?
questions.explanation: Genesis describes God creating Adam and then Eve.

questions.text: In Genesis 1, what did God say that brought light into existence?
questions.explanation: Genesis 1:3 records God saying, “Let there be light.”

questions.text: In Genesis 3, what did Eve do with the fruit after taking it?
options: She gave it to Adam and he ate
questions.explanation: Genesis says she gave it to her husband with her, and he ate.

questions.text: In Genesis 4, who killed Abel?
questions.explanation: Genesis 4:8 says Cain rose up against Abel and killed him.

questions.text: In Genesis 9, what sign did God set in the cloud as a covenant reminder?
questions.explanation: Genesis says God set the rainbow in the cloud as a sign of the covenant.

questions.text: In Genesis 32, what new name was given to Jacob?
questions.explanation: Genesis 32:28 says his name would be called Israel.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 1, what job did Nehemiah have in the king’s court?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah says he was cupbearer to the king.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 2, which set of names matches the opponents who mocked the work?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 2:19 names Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 6, where did the enemies ask Nehemiah to meet (a trap)?
questions.explanation: They asked him to meet in the plain of Ono (Nehemiah refused).

questions.text: According to Nehemiah 6, how long did it take to finish the wall?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 6:15 says the wall was finished in 52 days.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 4, what did Nehemiah tell the people to fight for?
options: Their brothers, sons, daughters, wives, and houses
questions.explanation: Nehemiah urged them to fight for their families and homes.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 8, what phrase is given as a reason not to grieve?
options: “The joy of the LORD is your strength.”
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 8:10 contains this line.

questions.text: In Genesis 1, what did God make on the fourth day?
questions.explanation: Genesis says God made lights in the expanse of the sky on the fourth day.

questions.text: In Genesis 2, what did God do on the seventh day?
questions.explanation: Genesis says God rested on the seventh day.

questions.text: In Genesis 11, why did the LORD confuse the people’s language?
options: So they wouldn’t understand one another’s speech and be scattered
So they would find the ark
questions.explanation: Genesis 11 says God confused their language and scattered them.

questions.text: In Mark 1, what did John the Baptizer wear?
options: Camel’s hair and a leather belt
questions.explanation: Mark describes John’s clothing as camel’s hair with a leather belt.

questions.text: In Mark 1, what did John the Baptizer eat?
questions.explanation: Mark 1:6 says his food was locusts and wild honey.

questions.text: In Mark 1, what did the unclean spirit call Jesus?
questions.explanation: Mark 1:24 records the spirit saying, “I know who you are: the Holy One of God.”

questions.text: In Matthew 1, what name did the angel tell Joseph to give Mary’s child?
questions.explanation: Matthew 1:21 says, “You shall call his name Jesus.”

questions.text: In Matthew 1, what does the name “Immanuel” mean?
questions.explanation: Matthew explains Immanuel as “God with us.”

questions.text: In Luke 2, what sign did the angels give the shepherds?
options: A baby wrapped in cloths and lying in a manger
questions.explanation: Luke 2:12 gives this sign to the shepherds.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 4, what did the people do in response to the threat?
options: Prayed to God and set a guard day and night
Asked the king to rebuild for them
questions.explanation: Nehemiah says they prayed and set a watch.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 8, from when until when was the Book of the Law read to the people?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 8:3 says it was read from early morning until midday.

questions.text: In Esther 2:7, what was Esther’s original name?
questions.explanation: Esther 2:7 says her name was Hadassah.

questions.text: In Esther 4, how long did Esther ask the Jews to fast?
questions.explanation: Esther 4:16 asks for a three-day fast.

questions.text: In Esther 5, what did the king extend to Esther when she approached?
questions.explanation: Esther 5:2 says the king held out the golden scepter.

questions.text: In Esther 5:14, how tall was the gallows Haman prepared for Mordecai?
questions.explanation: Esther 5:14 states it was fifty cubits high.

questions.text: In Genesis 12, how old was Abram when he departed from Haran?
questions.explanation: Genesis 12:4 says Abram was 75 years old when he departed from Haran.

questions.text: In Genesis 37, in Joseph’s dream, how many stars bowed down to him?
questions.explanation: Genesis 37:9 mentions the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowing down.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 4, how did the builders work while staying ready to defend?
options: They worked with one hand and held a weapon with the other
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 4:17 describes this exact detail.

questions.text: In Nehemiah 2:19, which names are listed as opponents who mocked the work?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah 2:19 names Sanballat, Tobiah, and Geshem.

questions.text: In John 1:1, what does the verse say about the Word?
options: The Word was with God, and the Word was God
The Word was created by angels
The Word was only a prophet
The Word was a song in the temple
questions.explanation: John 1:1 states the Word was with God and was God.

questions.text: Does Matthew state the monetary value of the wise men’s gifts?
options: No, the value is not stated
Yes, it gives an exact number
Yes, it lists the market price
Yes, it gives the value in denarii
questions.explanation: Matthew lists the gifts, but does not state a monetary value.

questions.text: The period often called the “400 years” between the Old and New Testaments is commonly known as what?
questions.explanation: Many call it the Intertestamental Period (a historical term).
questions.learnMore: This label is a historical term; it isn’t a Bible book title.
sources: General Bible history term: Intertestamental period

questions.text: Most of the New Testament was originally written in what language?
questions.explanation: Most NT books were written in Koine Greek.
questions.learnMore: A few phrases/words are Aramaic/Hebrew, but manuscripts are mainly Greek.

questions.text: The title “Christ” is related to which meaning?
questions.explanation: Christ comes from Greek “Christos,” meaning “anointed.”
questions.learnMore: It parallels the Hebrew concept behind “Messiah.”

questions.text: In Greek New Testament manuscripts, the name “Jesus” is commonly written as what?
questions.explanation: A common Greek form is Ἰησοῦς (Iēsous).
questions.learnMore: This is a linguistic history fact rather than a single-verse statement.

questions.text: In Hebrew studies, “YHWH” is often called what?
questions.explanation: YHWH is commonly called the Tetragrammaton (“four letters”).
questions.learnMore: This is a linguistic term used in Hebrew studies.
In 586 BC the Babylonian Empire destroyed Jerusalem and the temple, taking many people from Judah into exile.
questions.text: Which empire destroyed Jerusalem and the temple in 586 BC?
questions.explanation: The Babylonian Empire under King Nebuchadnezzar destroyed Jerusalem in 586 BC and carried many people from Judah into exile.

questions.learnMore: Daniel was a young man from Judah taken to Babylon, where he served in the royal court and remained faithful to God.
questions.text: Which prophet was taken to Babylon and served in the royal court during the exile?
questions.explanation: Daniel was taken to Babylon along with other young men from Judah and served in the royal court there.

questions.learnMore: The Persian Empire under Cyrus the Great conquered Babylon and allowed Jewish exiles to return and rebuild.
questions.text: Which empire defeated Babylon and allowed many Jewish exiles to return home?
questions.explanation: The Persians conquered Babylon and King Cyrus allowed Jewish exiles to return to their land.

questions.learnMore: Nehemiah led the effort to rebuild the walls of Jerusalem after the exile, working despite strong opposition.
questions.text: Who led the rebuilding of Jerusalem's walls after the exile?
questions.explanation: Nehemiah, a cupbearer to the Persian king, returned to Jerusalem and led the rebuilding of the city's walls.

questions.learnMore: Caesar Augustus ordered a census of the Roman world, which brought Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem.
questions.text: Which ruler ordered the census that brought Joseph and Mary to Bethlehem?
questions.explanation: Luke records that Caesar Augustus ordered a census, causing Joseph and Mary to travel to Bethlehem.

questions.learnMore: Many historians speak of about four hundred silent years between the final Old Testament writings and the coming of Christ.
sources: General Bible history term: Intertestamental period
questions.text: Roughly how many years are often said to lie between the Old and New Testaments (the Intertestamental Period)?
questions.explanation: The period between the last Old Testament writings and the coming of Christ is often rounded to about four hundred years.

questions.learnMore: The Maccabean family led a revolt against Seleucid rule, which is remembered in the celebration of Hanukkah.
questions.text: Which Jewish family led a revolt that is remembered in the celebration of Hanukkah?
questions.explanation: The Maccabees led a revolt against oppressive rule in the second century BC, an event connected with Hanukkah.

questions.learnMore: By the time of the New Testament, Koine Greek was widely used around the eastern Mediterranean and became the language of the New Testament.
questions.text: Which language became widely used around the eastern Mediterranean and is the main language of the New Testament?
questions.explanation: The New Testament was written mainly in Koine Greek, the common Greek of the eastern Roman world.

questions.learnMore: The Pharisees were a Jewish group in Jesus' day known for strict attention to the law and to oral traditions.
questions.text: Which Jewish group in Jesus' day was known for strict attention to the law and to traditions of the elders?
questions.explanation: The Pharisees emphasized careful observance of the law and traditions, often appearing in the Gospels in debates with Jesus.

questions.learnMore: During Jesus' earthly ministry, Judea and Galilee were under the control of the Roman Empire.
questions.text: Which empire ruled over Judea during Jesus' earthly ministry?
questions.explanation: The Roman Empire controlled Judea in the first century, with local rulers such as Herod and Roman governors like Pilate.

questions.learnMore: Antioch became an important center of early Christian mission, and it was here that believers were first called Christians.
questions.text: In which city were followers of Jesus first called 'Christians'?
questions.explanation: Acts records that the disciples were first called Christians in Antioch.

questions.learnMore: In AD 70 the Romans destroyed Jerusalem and the temple, a major turning point in Jewish and early Christian history.
questions.text: Around what year did the Romans destroy Jerusalem and its temple?
questions.explanation: The Roman army destroyed Jerusalem and the temple in AD 70.

questions.text: Who is described as writing the former book addressed to Theophilus?
questions.explanation: Luke is identified as the writer of the former book, which refers to the Gospel of Luke, addressed to Theophilus (Acts 1:1).

questions.text: To whom did Jesus appear after His resurrection before ascending to heaven?
questions.explanation: Jesus appeared to His disciples after His resurrection, as mentioned in Acts 1:3, giving them many convincing proofs.

questions.text: How many days after His resurrection did Jesus tell the disciples to wait in Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: Jesus instructed the disciples to wait for the Holy Spirit in Jerusalem until Pentecost, which occurs 50 days after Passover, but Jesus was with them for about 40 days after His resurrection (Acts 1:4-5).

questions.text: What did Jesus promise the apostles regarding the Spirit?
questions.explanation: Jesus promised that they would receive power when the Holy Spirit came upon them (Acts 1:8).

questions.text: What was the disciples' main question to Jesus before He ascended?
options: When will the kingdom be restored?
questions.explanation: The disciples asked Jesus if He was going to restore the kingdom to Israel, which relates to their understanding of His messianic mission (Acts 1:6).

questions.text: What visible sign accompanied Jesus' ascension into heaven?
questions.explanation: The disciples saw Jesus taken up into a cloud, which is a sign of His ascent into heaven (Acts 1:9).

questions.text: Who watched Jesus ascend into heaven?
questions.explanation: The disciples were the witnesses to Jesus' ascension, as stated in Acts 1:10-11.

questions.text: What instructions did the angels give the disciples after Jesus' ascension?
questions.explanation: The angels instructed the disciples to stay in Jerusalem and wait for the Holy Spirit (Acts 1:4-5, 11).

questions.text: Who was the Roman centurion described in Acts 10?
questions.explanation: Cornelius was the centurion in Acts 10:1-2. He is described as a devout man who feared God.

questions.text: What kind of vision did Peter have in Acts 10?
questions.explanation: Peter saw a vision of a sheet containing all kinds of animals in Acts 10:11-12. This vision prepared him to accept Gentiles.

questions.text: What message did the angel deliver to Cornelius?
questions.explanation: The angel told Cornelius to send men to Joppa to fetch Peter (Acts 10:5-6).

questions.text: How did Peter explain his change of view about Gentiles?
options: God showed him in a vision
questions.explanation: Peter explained that God gave him a vision showing no person should be considered unclean (Acts 10:19-20, 28).

questions.text: What response did the Gentiles have upon hearing Peter's message?
options: They believed and received the Holy Spirit
questions.explanation: The Gentiles believed Peter’s message and received the Holy Spirit, demonstrating God's acceptance of them (Acts 10:44-45).

questions.text: What was Peter's realization about God's plan in Acts 10?
questions.explanation: Peter realized that God shows no favoritism but accepts every nation who fears Him and does what is right (Acts 10:34-35).

questions.text: What command did Peter give to the new believers at the end of his visit?
options: To be baptized in Jesus' name
questions.explanation: Peter instructed them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ (Acts 10:48), signifying acceptance and repentance.

questions.text: What was the main reason the apostles in Jerusalem criticized Peter in Acts 11?
options: He visited the house of a Roman
questions.explanation: The apostles criticized Peter because he ate with Gentiles, which was seen as a violation of Jewish customs (Acts 11:2-3).

questions.text: How did Peter explain his actions to the apostles in Jerusalem?
options: He had a divine vision from God
questions.explanation: Peter explained that he received a vision from God that showed him not to call any person unclean (Acts 11:4-10), leading him to visit Cornelius's house.

questions.text: Who did Peter meet in Caesarea that led to the Gentile conversion?
questions.explanation: Peter met Cornelius, a Roman centurion, who was seeking God's guidance and became a believer (Acts 11:13-14).

questions.text: What was the reaction of the believers in Judea upon hearing about the Gentiles' turning to God?
options: They sent Peter back to Jerusalem
questions.explanation: The believers praised God because they recognized that salvation had also come to the Gentiles (Acts 11:18).

questions.text: What did the Christians in Antioch begin to do after hearing of the Gentile conversions?
questions.explanation: They were the first to be called Christians, signifying their identification with Christ (Acts 11:26).

questions.text: Which prophet was sent to Antioch to encourage the believers?
questions.explanation: Agabus prophesied about a coming famine and encouraged the believers to help others, showing the Spirit's guidance (Acts 11:27-28).

questions.text: What role did Barnabas play in Antioch according to Acts 11?
options: He encouraged believers and brought Saul
questions.explanation: Barnabas encouraged the new believers in Antioch and brought Saul (Paul) to help teach and strengthen the church (Acts 11:22-26).

questions.text: Who was the king that persecuted the church in Acts 12?
questions.explanation: Herod Agrippa I is the king mentioned in Acts 12 who persecuted the church, as described in verse 1.

questions.text: What action did Herod take against James in Acts 12?
questions.explanation: Herod imprisoned James, as recorded in Acts 12:1-2, which led to his execution.

questions.text: Why did Herod arrest Peter, according to Acts 12?
questions.explanation: Herod arrested Peter to please the Jewish leaders, as he was actively persecuting the church (Acts 12:3-4).

questions.text: How was Peter kept in prison while awaiting trial?
questions.explanation: Peter was chained between two soldiers, with guards stationed outside the prison, as described in Acts 12:6-7.

questions.text: What was the believers' response when they learned Peter was in prison?
questions.explanation: The believers gathered together and prayed earnestly for Peter’s release (Acts 12:5).

questions.text: How did Peter escape from prison?
questions.explanation: An angel of the Lord appeared, led Peter out of prison, and he was freed without harm (Acts 12:7-10).

questions.text: What was Herod’s reaction after Peter’s escape?
questions.explanation: Herod was furious that Peter had escaped, and he continued his persecution (Acts 12:19).

questions.text: What ultimately happened to Herod in Acts 12?
options: He was struck down and died
questions.explanation: Herod was struck down by an angel and died because he did not give glory to God, as described in Acts 12:23.

questions.text: Who were the prophets and teachers mentioned in Acts 13?
questions.explanation: Barnabas and Saul are identified as prophets and teachers in Acts 13:1, leading the church in Antioch.

questions.text: What specific command did the Holy Spirit give to set apart Barnabas and Saul?
questions.explanation: The Holy Spirit instructed the church to set apart Barnabas and Saul for the work to which He had called them, as seen in Acts 13:2.

questions.text: Where did Barnabas and Saul go after fasting and praying?
questions.explanation: They sent them off to Cyprus, the island where their first missionary journey took place (Acts 13:4).

questions.text: What was the primary purpose of the journey of Barnabas and Saul?
questions.explanation: Their mission was to spread the Gospel, as demonstrated by their activities and message (Acts 13:4-12).

questions.text: Who was Elymas in Acts 13?
questions.explanation: Elymas was a sorcerer and false prophet who tried to oppose Saul and Barnabas (Acts 13:8).

questions.text: What was the result of Saul (Paul) confronting Elymas?
questions.explanation: Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, caused Elymas to become blind for a time (Acts 13:11).

questions.text: What was the main message Paul preached in Pisidian Antioch?
questions.explanation: Paul focused on God's promise to Israel, highlighting Jesus as the fulfillment of that promise (Acts 13:32-33).

questions.text: How did the crowd in Pisidian Antioch initially respond to Paul and Barnabas' message?
questions.explanation: The crowd was initially amazed but then skeptical, leading to opposition from some (Acts 13:42-45).

questions.text: Where did Paul and Barnabas go after their initial arrival in Iconium?
questions.explanation: Acts 14:6 states that Paul and Barnabas traveled to Lystra and Derbe after their first stop in Iconium.

questions.text: What miracle did Paul perform in Lystra that amazed the people?
questions.explanation: In Acts 14:8–10, Paul heals a man who had been lame from birth, prompting the crowd's amazement.

questions.text: How did the people of Lystra initially react to the miracle performed by Paul?
questions.explanation: Acts 14:11–13 describes how the crowd called Barnabas Zeus and Paul Hermes, trying to worship them as gods.

options: They tore their clothes and denied it
questions.explanation: Acts 14:14–15 shows that Paul and Barnabas tore their clothes and fervently told the people they were human, urging them to turn to the true God.

options: They stirred up the crowd and stoned Paul
questions.explanation: Acts 14:19 describes how some Jews from Antioch and Iconium stirred up the crowd and stoned Paul, thinking they had killed him.

options: They gathered around Paul and he got up
questions.explanation: Acts 14:20 states that after the disciples gathered around him, Paul got up and went into the city.
Acts 14:21 indicates that Paul and Barnabas preached the gospel and strengthened the disciples, establishing churches in these cities.

questions.text: What was the main reason for the dispute in Acts 15?
options: The timing of the next missionary journey
How to handle disputes among leaders
The best way to collect offerings
questions.explanation: The dispute arose over whether Gentile believers needed to be circumcised and follow the Law of Moses (Acts 15:1).

questions.text: Who was chosen along with Paul and Barnabas to go to Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: Judas (Barsabbas) was chosen as a delegate along with Paul and Barnabas to go to Jerusalem (Acts 15:22).

questions.text: What was the outcome of the Jerusalem council?
options: They decided all Gentiles must be circumcised
They issued a gentile exemption from most Jewish laws
They postponed the decision to a later meeting
They sent Peter to negotiate with the Jews
questions.explanation: They concluded Gentiles did not need to be circumcised and should abstain from certain practices, easing the requirements (Acts 15:19-20).

questions.text: Which letter was sent to the churches regarding Gentile believers?
options: A letter confirming they should avoid specific practices
A warning to convert to Judaism
A request to send additional leaders
questions.explanation: The letter confirmed that Gentiles should avoid practices like idolatry, illicit marriage, and eating meat sacrificed to idols (Acts 15:23-29).

questions.text: How did Paul and Barnabas respond after the meeting?
options: They refused to accept the decision
questions.explanation: Paul and Barnabas set out to deliver the letter and the decision of the council to the churches (Acts 15:30-32).

questions.text: What was the main theme of Acts 15?
options: The spread of Christianity to Europe
questions.explanation: Acts 15 centers on the debate regarding whether Gentile believers should follow Jewish laws, focusing on their inclusion into the church (Acts 15).

questions.text: Who is said to have given the decision and the letter in Acts 15?
questions.explanation: James, the leader of the Jerusalem church, gave the conclusion and the letter to be sent to the churches (Acts 15:13-21).

questions.text: What role did the Holy Spirit play in Acts 15?
options: It was not mentioned in this chapter
It convinced Paul to start his journey
questions.explanation: The Holy Spirit was involved in guiding the leaders, especially in deciding that Gentiles should not be burdened with all Jewish laws (Acts 15:28-29).

questions.text: Where did Paul and Silas travel to in Acts 16?
questions.explanation: Paul and Silas traveled to Philippi, as noted in Acts 16:12, where they encountered Lydia.

questions.text: Who was the woman that Lydia was, according to Acts 16?
questions.explanation: Lydia was described as a seller of purple fabric, which was a wealthy and influential trade, in Acts 16:14.

questions.text: What happened to Paul and Silas in prison?
questions.explanation: An earthquake shook the prison, opening the doors and loosing the chains, as described in Acts 16:26.

questions.text: How did the jailer respond after the earthquake?
options: He was about to harm himself
questions.explanation: The jailer was about to harm himself, fearing the prisoners had escaped, but Paul reassured him in Acts 16:27-28.

questions.text: What was the jailer’s response after Paul and Silas shared the Gospel?
options: He told them to leave quietly
He and his family were baptized
questions.explanation: The jailer and his household were baptized after hearing the Gospel, as recorded in Acts 16:33-34.

questions.text: Which command did Paul give to the spirit possessing the girl?
questions.explanation: Paul commanded the spirit to come out of the girl, as in Acts 16:18.

questions.text: Why were Paul and Silas imprisoned in Philippi?
questions.explanation: They cast out the spirit from the girl, which upset her owners and led to their imprisonment, as described in Acts 16:16-24.

questions.text: What was the primary theme demonstrated through Paul and Silas’s experience in prison?
questions.explanation: Their unwavering praise and prayer in prison exemplify trusting God even during suffering, as shown in Acts 16:25.

questions.text: In Acts 17, where did Paul and Silas first go to teach about Jesus in Thessalonica?
questions.explanation: Paul and Silas initially went to the Jewish synagogue to reason with the people (Acts 17:1).

questions.text: How did some Jews react to Paul’s message in Thessalonica?
questions.explanation: Some Jews believed and joined Paul and Silas, which is highlighted as a positive response (Acts 17:4).

questions.text: What was the main place Paul went to in Athens to share the gospel?
questions.explanation: Paul addressed the Athenians at the Areopagus, a significant council and gathering place (Acts 17:22-23).

questions.text: What Greek idol did Paul notice in Athens that prompted him to speak about the true God?
options: An idol to an unknown god
questions.explanation: Paul saw an altar with an inscription to 'an unknown god,' which he used to introduce the true God (Acts 17:23).

questions.text: How did some in Athens respond to Paul’s teachings about resurrection?
options: They took him to the council
questions.explanation: Some mocked Paul when he spoke about the resurrection, showing skepticism among the Athenians (Acts 17:32).

questions.text: What was a significant reason for Paul’s visit to Athens?
options: To observe and preach to the philosophers
questions.explanation: Paul went to Athens to observe the city and preach the gospel to the philosophers and people there (Acts 17:16-18).

questions.text: Who was Jason in Acts 17?
questions.explanation: Jason was a supporter of Paul and Silas, hosting them at his home (Acts 17:5-7).

questions.text: What did Paul ultimately urge the Athenians to do with the message of Jesus?
questions.explanation: Paul called for repentance and believing in the one true God, emphasizing the importance of faith (Acts 17:30-31).

questions.text: Where does Paul meet Aquila and Priscilla in Acts 18?
questions.explanation: Paul meets Aquila and Priscilla in Corinth, as recorded in Acts 18:1-3, where he stays with them because they share the same trade of tentmaking.

questions.text: What occupation did Paul and Aquila share?
questions.explanation: Paul and Aquila were tentmakers, which is detailed in Acts 18:3, where they worked together to support themselves.

questions.text: How did Paul begin his ministry in Corinth according to Acts 18?
questions.explanation: Paul first engaged in preaching in the synagogue in Corinth, as described in Acts 18:4, following his standard practice of starting gospel work there.

questions.text: What vision did Paul receive in Corinth?
options: A vision of a man from Macedonia
questions.explanation: Paul received a vision of a man from Macedonia calling him to preach there, which is described in Acts 18:9-10, encouraging him to stay in Corinth.

questions.text: What was the main message Paul preached in Corinth?
questions.explanation: While in Corinth, Paul preached about Jesus and the necessity of repentance and faith, as summarized in Acts 18:8, where many believed in the Lord.

questions.text: Who opposed Paul’s work in Corinth?
questions.explanation: Some Jews opposed Paul and brought him before the judgment seat, as described in Acts 18:12, which led to his defense before Gallio.

questions.text: What action did Gallio take regarding the accusations against Paul?
questions.explanation: Gallio dismissed the case against Paul, stating it was a matter of Jewish disputes, which prevented Paul from being punished, as in Acts 18:14-16.

questions.text: How did the synagogue react to Silas and Timothy in Corinth?
questions.explanation: The synagogue was receptive, and many believed after Paul’s teachings, especially as Silas and Timothy joined him, encouraging the believers, as seen in Acts 18:8.

questions.text: What did Paul do after arriving in Ephesus and finding some disciples?
options: He baptized them in the name of Jesus
He preached in the synagogues only
He immediately began teaching directly in the temple
He sent for the apostles in Jerusalem
questions.explanation: Paul found disciples and baptized them in the name of Jesus, then laid hands on them to receive the Holy Spirit (Acts 19:1-6).

questions.text: What was the sign that the new disciples had received the Holy Spirit?
options: They spoke in tongues and prophesied
They began to perform miracles publicly
questions.explanation: The disciples spoke in tongues and prophesied as evidence of receiving the Holy Spirit (Acts 19:6).

questions.text: What reaction did the local craftsmen have to Paul’s teachings about Jesus?
options: They gathered a meeting to oppose him
They tried to buy his influence
questions.explanation: The craftsmen, especially those making silver shrines, gathered against Paul because his teachings threatened their trade (Acts 19:23-27).

questions.text: What name did the evil spirit recognize when confronting the sons of Sceva?
questions.explanation: The evil spirit recognized Jesus and Paul, but not Sceva, highlighting that only Jesus' name has authority over spirits (Acts 19:13-16).

questions.text: What did many who practiced magic do after hearing about the Lord’s power?
questions.explanation: Many magicians publicly burned their scrolls, showing their repentance and turning away from magic (Acts 19:19).

questions.text: What was the outcome of Paul’s ministry in Ephesus in terms of impact?
options: Many conversions and a decline in magic practices
Paul left quickly without any activity
questions.explanation: Many came to faith in Jesus, and magic practices declined as people burned their scrolls (Acts 19:17-20).

questions.text: What was the result of the riot caused by the silversmiths?
options: The crowd calmed and peace returned
The city ruler intervened and silenced the crowd
questions.explanation: The city clerk addressed the crowd, calming them and preventing violence (Acts 19:35-41).

questions.text: What did the disciples do after the riot and Paul’s departure?
questions.explanation: After these events, the disciples continued to preach the gospel boldly, exemplifying perseverance (Acts 19:20).

questions.text: On which day did the events of Acts 2 occur?
questions.explanation: Acts 2 describes the day of Pentecost, a Jewish festival celebrated 50 days after Passover (Acts 2:1).

questions.text: What event caused the disciples to speak in different languages?
options: The Holy Spirit came upon them
questions.explanation: The Holy Spirit enabled the disciples to speak in other languages, as described in Acts 2:4.

questions.text: What did the crowd initially think was happening when they heard the disciples speaking?
questions.explanation: Some in the crowd thought the disciples were drunk, as noted in Acts 2:13.

questions.text: Who stood up and addressed the crowd after the Spirit came?
questions.explanation: Peter was the one who spoke to the crowd in Acts 2:14-15, explaining what was happening.

questions.text: What scripture did Peter quote to explain the events?
questions.explanation: Peter quotes Joel 2 about God's Spirit being poured out on all flesh (Acts 2:17-21).

questions.text: How did the people respond after Peter preached about Jesus?
questions.explanation: The crowd responded by repenting and being baptized, about 3,000 people (Acts 2:38-41).

questions.text: What was a key sign of the early church community described in Acts 2?
questions.options: Sharing possessions and breaking bread together
questions.explanation: Acts 2 describes believers sharing possessions and regularly gathering for meals and worship, emphasizing community (Acts 2:44-46).

questions.text: What miraculous sign accompanied the coming of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost?
questions.explanation: Tongues of fire appeared over the disciples' heads, symbolizing the Holy Spirit's arrival (Acts 2:3).

questions.text: What was Peter’s main message in his speech at Pentecost?
options: Moses led us out of Egypt
questions.explanation: Peter proclaimed that Jesus is Lord and Messiah, calling people to repent and be baptized (Acts 2:36-38).

questions.text: Where did Paul shed tears as he spoke to the elders?
questions.explanation: Paul shed tears while speaking to the elders from Ephesus, emphasizing his love and concern for them (Acts 20:17-19).

questions.text: What warning did Paul give to the elders regarding future hardships?
options: That savage wolves would come among them
questions.explanation: Paul warned that savage wolves would come among the flock, indicating upcoming challenges and false teachers (Acts 20:29).

questions.text: What did Paul commend the elders to do in their ministry?
options: Assist the weak and set an example
questions.explanation: Paul encouraged them to aid the weak and to set an example by their life and teachings (Acts 20:35).

questions.text: What did Paul do when he had finished speaking?
questions.explanation: After speaking, Paul knelt with all of them and prayed, demonstrating humility and prayerfulness (Acts 20:36).

questions.text: How long did Paul preach to the elders at Troas?
questions.explanation: Paul preached all night, showing his dedication to teaching and encouraging believers (Acts 20:9).

questions.text: What prophecy related to Paul’s future was mentioned in this chapter?
questions.explanation: The Holy Spirit indicated that Paul would testify in Rome, which he later fulfilled (Acts 23:11).

questions.text: From which city did Paul sail to Troas?
questions.explanation: Paul traveled from Macedonia and established a church in Philippi before arriving at Troas (Acts 20:1-6).

questions.text: What was Paul’s purpose in calling the elders together at Miletus?
options: To give a farewell farewell speech
To address their spiritual needs and warn them
questions.explanation: Paul called the elders to warn them about future dangers, encourage, and prepare them for their ministry (Acts 20:17-31).

questions.text: Where does Paul set sail from at the beginning of Acts 21?
questions.explanation: Paul leaves Cenchrea, a suburb of Corinth, as he begins his journey to Jerusalem (Acts 21:1).

questions.text: How do the travelers in Acts 21 respond to Paul’s warning about dangers in Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: Many people plead with Paul not to go to Jerusalem, fearing danger, but he is determined to go (Acts 21:12-13).

questions.text: Who is the prophet that warns Paul about his upcoming suffering?
questions.explanation: Agabus, the prophet, warns Paul through a symbolic gesture that he will be bound in Jerusalem (Acts 21:10-11).

questions.text: What action do the elders of Jerusalem take upon hearing Paul's report?
questions.explanation: The elders rejoice and glorify God because they see the Gentiles turning to salvation through Paul (Acts 21:19-20).

questions.text: What vow do Paul and his companions undertake in Acts 21?
questions.explanation: Paul participates in a Nazarite vow, which involved ritual purification and additional offerings, to demonstrate his respect for Jewish customs (Acts 21:23-24).

questions.text: What false accusation is made against Paul by the Jewish crowd in Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: The crowd accuses Paul of bringing Greeks into the temple and causing defilement, which leads to an attack (Acts 21:27-28).

questions.text: How does Paul respond to the accusations against him?
questions.explanation: Paul explains that he is a Jew from Tarsus and that he worships God according to the Jewish law, addressing their accusations (Acts 21:39-40).

questions.text: Who was speaking to the crowd in Acts 22?
questions.explanation: Paul was addressing the Jewish crowd in Jerusalem in Acts 22:1.

questions.text: What was Paul’s original name mentioned in Acts 22?
questions.explanation: Paul referred to himself as Saul in Acts 22:3, indicating his original Hebrew name.

questions.text: What was Paul doing before his conversion?
questions.explanation: In Acts 22:4, Paul states he persecuted Christians, which was his life before conversion.

questions.text: What miraculous event did Paul describe experiencing on the road to Damascus?
questions.explanation: Paul recounts hearing Jesus' voice in Acts 22:7-8 during his journey to Damascus, which led to his conversion.

questions.text: How did Paul respond when asked why he was persecuting Christians?
questions.explanation: Paul states in Acts 22:19-20 that he thought he was doing God's will, which drove his previous actions.

questions.text: Who is the high priest mentioned when Paul was recounting his story?
questions.explanation: Paul mentions Ananias as the high priest who questioned him (Acts 22:5).

questions.text: Why did the crowd become angry during Paul's speech?
questions.explanation: Some in the crowd became angry because Paul claimed that Jesus was the Christ, which upset them (implied by the context).

questions.text: What was Paul’s mission after his conversion, as he states in Acts 22?
questions.explanation: Paul declares that he was sent to the Gentiles in Acts 22:21, emphasizing his calling to reach non-Jews.

questions.text: Who did Paul address as 'brothers and fathers' in Acts 23:1?
questions.explanation: Paul addressed the Jewish council as 'brothers and fathers' in Acts 23:1. This was in his defense before the Sanhedrin, a Jewish ruling council (verse 1).

questions.text: What did Paul declare about his conduct before God in Acts 23:1?
questions.explanation: Paul stated he had lived blamelessly before God concerning his conduct in Acts 23:1, emphasizing his integrity in faithfulness and obedience (verse 1).

questions.text: What action did the conspiracy against Paul involve in Acts 23:12-14?
options: Swearing an oath to kill Paul
questions.explanation: In Acts 23:12-14, more than forty men bound themselves with an oath to not eat or drink until they had killed Paul, plotting an ambush against him (verses 12-14).

questions.text: How did the Roman commander discover the plot against Paul?
questions.explanation: Paul's nephew overheard the conspiracy and informed the Roman commander, leading to the plot's discovery (Acts 23:16).

questions.text: What method did the Roman soldiers use to protect Paul during the transfer?
questions.explanation: The Roman soldiers transferred Paul by night with a military escort to ensure his safety, as described in Acts 23:23-24.

questions.text: What reason did the Jewish leaders give for wanting to kill Paul after the plot was uncovered?
questions.explanation: They claimed they wanted to kill him due to his activities stirring up trouble and blasphemy, seeking his death in Acts 23:12-14 and subsequent verses.

questions.text: Which Roman official ordered Paul’s transfer to Caesarea in Acts 23?
questions.explanation: Centurion Claudius Lysias ordered Paul’s transfer to the governor in Caesarea for further judgment, as seen in Acts 23:23-24.

questions.text: How did Paul’s defense before the Sanhedrin reflect his confidence in God?
options: He declared he lived blamelessly before God
He admitted guilt to avoid punishment
questions.explanation: Paul stated he had lived blamelessly before God regarding his conduct, showing his trust that God judged his heart and actions (Acts 23:1).

questions.text: Who brought the accusations against Paul in Acts 24?
questions.explanation: Ananias the high priest and elders brought the claims against Paul (Acts 24:1).

questions.text: How did Paul defend himself before Felix?
options: He declared innocence and explained his faith
questions.explanation: Paul defended himself by asserting his innocence and explaining his faith (Acts 24:10-21).

questions.text: What did Paul say was his hope for the Jewish people?
questions.explanation: Paul spoke of his hope in the resurrection, which was a key part of his faith (Acts 24:15).

questions.text: What was Felix's response to Paul’s case?
options: He kept Paul in prison permanently
He postponed judgment, hoping for a bribe
questions.explanation: Felix postponed the decision, hoping to receive a bribe from Paul (Acts 24:26-27).

questions.text: How long did Felix keep Paul in custody?
questions.explanation: Felix kept Paul in custody for about two years before leaving him in prison (Acts 24:27).

questions.text: Who was the Roman officer in charge of Paul’s case?
questions.explanation: Julius was the Roman officer who guarded Paul and brought him to Felix (Acts 24:23).

questions.text: What was Paul’s main charge against the Jewish leaders?
questions.explanation: Paul pointed out that the main issue was their opposition to the hope of resurrection (Acts 24:15).

questions.text: What does Acts 24 reveal about Felix’s attitude towards Paul?
options: He was immediately convinced of Paul’s innocence
He was indifferent and postponed judgment
questions.explanation: Felix showed indifference by postponing judgment, hoping for a bribe (Acts 24:26).

questions.text: Who was the Roman governor addressing Paul in Acts 25?
questions.explanation: Festus is the Roman governor who addresses Paul in Acts 25:1-4, as he takes over the case from Felix.

questions.text: Why did Festus want to leave Jerusalem for Caesarea?
questions.explanation: Festus planned to hear from Jewish leaders regarding Paul’s case, which is why he traveled to Caesarea (Acts 25:2-3).

questions.text: How did Paul defend himself before Festus and Agrippa?
options: He explained his calling from Jesus
questions.explanation: Paul explained his conversion and mission from Jesus, emphasizing he is being persecuted unjustly (Acts 25:8-11).

questions.text: Who arrived to hear Paul’s case along with Festus and Agrippa?
questions.explanation: King Agrippa II was present to hear Paul’s defense, as described in Acts 25:13-22.

questions.text: What did Paul focus on in his speech before Agrippa?
questions.explanation: Paul recounts his conversion experience and calling from Jesus, which is the core of his speech (Acts 25:13-22).

questions.text: How did Agrippa respond to Paul’s defense?
options: He was almost convinced to become a Christian
He ordered Paul to be released
questions.explanation: Agrippa expressed that Paul’s case was persuasive enough that he was 'almost persuaded' to become a Christian (Acts 26:28).

questions.text: What was the outcome of Paul’s hearing before Agrippa and Festus?
options: He was acquitted of all charges
questions.explanation: Paul appealed his case to Caesar, which was his right as a Roman citizen, as stated in Acts 25:11 and 26:32.

questions.text: Who was the Apostle giving his defense in Acts 26?
questions.explanation: The Apostle Paul is the one defending himself before King Agrippa in Acts 26:1.

questions.text: What did Paul say was part of his former life before converting?
questions.explanation: Paul states in Acts 26:5 that he was a Pharisee, which is part of his background before his conversion.

questions.text: Which event does Paul mention as evidence of God's power in his life?
questions.explanation: Paul refers to his experience of conversion on the road to Damascus as evidence of God's power (Acts 26:12–15).

questions.text: What was Paul's mission according to Acts 26?
options: To bear witness to the Gentiles
questions.explanation: Paul states in Acts 26:17–18 that his mission is to open eyes and turn people from darkness to light, especially among the Gentiles.

questions.text: How did King Agrippa react to Paul's testimony?
questions.explanation: Agrippa responds that Paul almost persuades him to become a Christian (Acts 26:28).

questions.text: What was Paul’s reason for appealing to Caesar?
options: He wanted a trial in Rome
He was afraid of the Jews
questions.explanation: Paul explicitly states in Acts 26:32 that he appeals to Caesar to have his case heard in Rome.

questions.text: What does Paul say about the hope of his ancestors in Acts 26?
questions.explanation: Paul mentions that he hopes in the promise made to the ancestors—a reference to the resurrection (Acts 26:6–8).

questions.text: Who was in charge of the ship during Acts 27?
questions.explanation: Julius the Roman centurion was responsible for the ship, as stated in verse 1.

questions.text: Why did Paul warn the sailors about continuing their voyage?
options: They would face danger and loss
He was asked by the crew
questions.explanation: Paul warned of danger because he perceived that the voyage would be harmful and involve heavy weather (verse 10).

questions.text: What happened as a result of the storm?
options: The ship was shipwrecked on Malta
questions.explanation: The ship was caught in a violent storm and eventually wrecked on Malta (verses 41-44).

questions.text: What advice did Paul give to the crew during the storm?
options: Trust in God and stay on the ship
questions.explanation: Paul advised them to trust in God's promise and stay on the ship, which they did until it wrecked (verses 21-25).

questions.text: What occurred when the shipwrecked on Malta?
options: Paul was bitten by a snake
questions.explanation: Paul was bitten by a snake and remained unharmed, which led to the locals recognizing his special status (verses 28-34).

questions.text: What was the significance of Paul healing the man on Malta?
options: It demonstrated God's power through him
questions.explanation: Paul healing the father of Publius showed God's power working through him and increased his credibility among the inhabitants (verses 8-9).

questions.text: Where did Paul continue his journey after arriving in Malta?
questions.explanation: After Malta, Paul arrived in Rome to continue his mission and face trial, as detailed later in Acts 28.

questions.text: What does Acts 27 teach about trusting God amid difficulties?
questions.explanation: Paul’s reassurance to trust God's promise demonstrates the importance of faith and trust in God's plan during life's storms.

questions.text: Where does Paul finally arrive in Acts 28?
questions.explanation: Paul arrives in Rome, as detailed in Acts 28:14-16, fulfilling his journey to the imperial city.

questions.text: How do Paul and his companions reach Rome?
questions.explanation: Paul and his companions arrive in Rome via ship after a storm, as described in Acts 28:13-15.

questions.text: How does Paul first meet the local believers in Rome?
questions.explanation: Paul is welcomed into the believers’ home, which he first visits after arriving in Rome, as noted in Acts 28:14.

questions.text: What does Paul do when he arrives in Rome?
questions.explanation: Paul preaches both publicly and privately, sharing the message of Jesus, as the entire chapter depicts his ongoing ministry.

questions.text: What is the main message Paul proclaims in Rome?
questions.explanation: Paul emphasizes God's kingdom and the fulfillment of God's promises through Jesus, as seen throughout Acts 28.

questions.text: How long does Paul stay in Rome according to Acts 28?
questions.explanation: The chapter indicates that Paul stays under house arrest for about two years in Rome, sharing the gospel freely.

questions.text: What is significant about Paul’s preaching in Acts 28?
options: He preaches to Jews and Gentiles
questions.explanation: Paul preaches freely to both Jews and Gentiles, demonstrating the inclusive nature of the gospel, as emphasized in Acts 28.

questions.text: What is the tone of the overall narrative in Acts 28?
questions.explanation: The chapter ends with a tone of hope and opportunity for the Gospel's expansion, as Paul is allowed to preach freely.

questions.text: Who was sitting at the temple gate when Peter and John approached?
questions.explanation: Acts 3:2 describes a man who was lame from birth sitting at the temple gate called Beautiful. He was expecting to receive alms when Peter and John approached.

questions.text: What did Peter say to the man?
options: I have no gold or silver
In the name of Jesus Christ, get up!
Go to the temple and pray
questions.explanation: Acts 3:6 records Peter commanding the man, 'In the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, walk,' leading to his healing.

questions.text: What was the man able to do after being healed?
questions.explanation: Acts 3:8 describes the man jumping to his feet, walking, and entering the temple with Peter and John, showing his complete healing.

questions.text: How did the crowd react when they saw the healed man?
questions.explanation: Acts 3:10 notes that the crowd was filled with wonder and amazement at the healing of the man.

questions.text: What did Peter do after healing the man?
questions.explanation: Acts 3:11-16 depicts Peter addressing the people, explaining the miracle, and pointing to Jesus Christ as the source of healing.

questions.text: What did Peter tell the people about their need for repentance?
options: To turn to God and repent
questions.explanation: Acts 3:19 encourages repentance so that times of refreshing may come from the Lord, emphasizing the importance of turning back to God.

questions.text: Which prophet did Peter mention in his speech to explain Jesus' role?
questions.explanation: Peter cites Moses in Acts 3:22-23, confirming that Jesus was the fulfillment of the prophets’ promises.

questions.text: What was the main message Peter emphasized in this chapter?
questions.explanation: Throughout Acts 3, Peter emphasizes that faith in Jesus Christ is the source of the miracle and salvation for all who believe.

questions.text: What are Peter and John doing when they are arrested in Acts 4?
questions.explanation: Peter and John are arrested while they are preaching about Jesus, specifically after healing a man in Acts 3 and continuing to teach in Acts 4:1-2.

questions.text: How does Peter respond when asked by the rulers about healing the man?
questions.explanation: Peter boldly declares that healing was through the name of Jesus Christ, emphasizing faith in Jesus’ name (Acts 4:10-12).

questions.text: What key message does Peter share about salvation in Acts 4?
questions.explanation: Peter states that salvation is found only in Jesus Christ, highlighting his role as the only name given by which people can be saved (Acts 4:12).

questions.text: How do the believers respond to the threats and opposition?
questions.explanation: The believers pray for boldness to continue preaching despite opposition, as recorded in Acts 4:29-30.

questions.text: What common attitude do the believers show in Acts 4 regarding possessions?
options: They sell their possessions and share
They give everything to the church building fund
questions.explanation: The believers demonstrate unity and generosity by selling possessions and sharing with those in need (Acts 4:32-35).

questions.text: What was one notable effect of the believers' unity and prayer in Acts 4?
questions.explanation: After their prayer, the place was shaken, and they were filled with the Holy Spirit, enabling boldness and miracles (Acts 4:31).

questions.text: What was the reaction of the leaders toward Peter and John after their arrest?
questions.explanation: The leaders threatened the apostles and commanded them not to speak about Jesus anymore (Acts 4:17-18).

questions.text: What was the sin of Ananias and Sapphira in Acts 5?
options: They lied about their giving to the apostles
They refused to share their possessions
They argued openly in the church
questions.explanation: Ananias and Sapphira lied about the amount they received from selling land, claiming they contributed all when they secretly kept part of it (Acts 5:1-2).

questions.text: How did Peter respond when Ananias lied about the sale?
options: He questioned him about the price
He accused him of lying to God
He told him to confess to the elders
questions.explanation: Peter confronted Ananias, telling him he lied not just to men, but to God (Acts 5:3-4).

questions.text: What was the consequence for Ananias after he lied?
options: He was healed of his disease
questions.explanation: Ananias fell dead instantly after Peter confronted him about his lie (Acts 5:5).

questions.text: What happened to Sapphira after she lied about her part of the sale?
options: She was forgiven and allowed to stay
She also died suddenly, like her husband
questions.explanation: Sapphira fell dead instantly after lying, confirming her husband's fate (Acts 5:10).

questions.text: How did the apostles respond to the deaths of Ananias and Sapphira?
options: They were filled with fear and honored God
questions.explanation: The apostles and believers were filled with fear and continued to revere God’s authority (Acts 5:11).

questions.text: What was a major effect of the events in Acts 5 on the church?
options: More believers were added to the Lord
questions.explanation: Despite the fear, the Lord added more believers to the church (Acts 5:14).

questions.text: What was the disciples' attitude toward suffering in Acts 5?
options: They avoided suffering at all costs
They rejoiced that they were counted worthy to suffer
questions.explanation: The believers counted it a privilege to suffer for Jesus, rejoicing in their persecution (Acts 5:41).

questions.text: What role did the apostles play in Acts 5 after their release from prison?
options: They continued to teach boldly in the temple
questions.explanation: The apostles continued teaching and proclaiming Jesus boldly in the temple after their release (Acts 5:20).

questions.text: What was the main issue in the early church that led to the appointment of deacons in Acts 6?
questions.explanation: The apostles appointed deacons to oversee the daily distribution to widows, as stated in Acts 6:1.

questions.text: Who was chosen as one of the first deacons in Acts 6?
questions.explanation: Nicanor was one of the men chosen, along with others including Stephen, as mentioned in Acts 6:5.

questions.text: Why were the apostles initially hesitant to leave the ministry of prayer and the word to serve tables?
options: They needed to focus on prayer and ministry of the word
They didn’t trust the other men
They were too busy building the church
questions.explanation: The apostles emphasized their calling to prayer and the ministry of the word, which is why they prioritized these tasks over serving tables, as seen in Acts 6:4.

questions.text: What was Stephen known for among the early believers?
options: Being full of faith and the Holy Spirit
questions.explanation: Stephen was described as a man full of faith and the Holy Spirit, and he was known for his service and divine favor, as in Acts 6:5-8.

questions.text: What caused the opposition to Stephen that led to his arrest?
options: He spoke against the temple and the law
He caused a riot at the synagogue
questions.explanation: Stephen's speech accused some members of opposing God's law and resisting the Holy Spirit, which led to accusations and his arrest (Acts 6:13-14).

questions.text: How did Stephen respond when accused of speaking against Moses and God?
options: He retold the history of Israel and accused them
questions.explanation: Stephen responded by recounting Israel's history and indicting those opposing God's messengers, as described in Acts 7, which included his speech before they stoned him.

questions.text: What was Stephen's fate after his speech in Acts 7?
questions.explanation: Stephen was brutally stoned, making him the first martyr recorded in the church, as detailed in Acts 7:58-60.

questions.text: What example did Stephen set at the moment of his death?
questions.explanation: Stephen prayed for his persecutors, saying, 'Lord, do not charge them with this sin,' demonstrating forgiveness and faith even in death (Acts 7:60).

questions.text: Who was the high priest addressed by Stephen in Acts 7?
questions.explanation: Stephen was brought before Caiaphas, the high priest, as indicated in verse 1.

questions.text: What does Stephen accuse the Israelites of doing to prophets?
questions.explanation: Stephen accuses the Israelites of murdering the prophets, as seen in verses 52-53.

questions.text: Where did Stephen say Abraham was called from?
questions.explanation: Stephen mentions that God called Abraham from Ur of the Chaldeans (verse 2).

questions.text: What did Moses see at the burning bush?
options: A burning bush that was not consumed
questions.explanation: Stephen describes Moses seeing a burning bush that was not consumed (verse 31).

questions.text: What was Stephen’s main charge against the Jewish leaders?
options: They rejected the Holy Spirit and murdered Jesus
questions.explanation: Stephen accuses the leaders of rejecting the Spirit and killing Jesus, the Righteous One (verses 51-52).

questions.text: How did Stephen respond when accused of speaking against Moses and God?
questions.explanation: Stephen responded by recounting Israel’s history and their repeated disobedience, showing how they resisted the Holy Spirit (verses 51-52).

questions.text: What was Stephen's attitude towards his accusers during his speech?
questions.explanation: Stephen demonstrates faithfulness and courage, speaking boldly despite knowing his fate dangers (verse 55).

questions.text: What was Stephen doing as he was dying?
questions.explanation: Stephen prayed for his killers, saying 'Lord, do not hold this sin against them' (verse 60).

questions.text: What was the name of the city where Philip preached to the Ethiopian eunuch?
questions.explanation: Philip preached to the Ethiopian eunuch in Gaza, as indicated in Acts 8:26-27.

questions.text: Who was persecuting the early church, leading to many Christians fleeing Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: Saul was persecuting the church, causing believers to scatter, as described in Acts 8:1-3.

questions.text: How did Philip know where to go to meet the Ethiopian eunuch?
options: He was told by an angel
He saw him in a vision
questions.explanation: The Holy Spirit directed Philip to go to the chariot, as mentioned in Acts 8:29.

questions.text: What was the eunuch reading when Philip approached him?
questions.explanation: The eunuch was reading from the scroll of Isaiah when Philip approached, as stated in Acts 8:28.

questions.text: What was the significant question Philip asked the eunuch?
options: Do you understand what you read?
questions.explanation: Philip asked if he understood what he was reading, prompting the eunuch to seek explanation (Acts 8:30-31).

questions.text: What was the response of the eunuch after Philip explained the scripture?
questions.explanation: The eunuch believed and was baptized as a public declaration of his faith, shown in Acts 8:36-38.

questions.text: What happened immediately after the eunuch’s baptism?
questions.explanation: The Spirit suddenly took Philip away, and the eunuch continued on his way, as described at the end of the story in Acts 8:39-40.

questions.text: What is the main theme of Acts 8?
questions.explanation: Acts 8 highlights how persecution led to the spread of the gospel beyond Jerusalem, especially through Philip’s missionary work.

questions.text: Who was the disciple known for persecuting Christians before his conversion?
questions.explanation: Saul was the one persecuting Christians prior to his conversion on the road to Damascus, as described in Acts 9:1-2.

questions.text: What was Saul traveling to do when he encountered Jesus?
questions.explanation: Saul was on his way to persecute Christians in Damascus, as the narrative states in Acts 9:1-2.

questions.text: What bright light caused Saul to fall to the ground?
questions.explanation: Saul saw a bright light from heaven that caused him to fall to the ground (Acts 9:3-4).

questions.text: What did Jesus ask Saul during his encounter?
questions.explanation: Jesus asked Saul, 'Why are you persecuting me?' indicating his connection to the church (Acts 9:4-5).

questions.text: What was Saul's initial response to Jesus' question?
questions.explanation: Saul responded, 'Who are you, Lord?', recognizing the voice as Jesus' (Acts 9:5).

questions.text: What was Saul told to do after his encounter?
options: Go into the city and wait
questions.explanation: Jesus told Saul to go into the city and wait for further instructions (Acts 9:6).

questions.text: Who was Saul's companion who saw the light but did not hear Jesus' voice?
questions.explanation: Saul's companions saw the light but did not hear the voice, as described in Acts 9:7.

questions.text: What was Saul doing when a disciple named Ananias was sent to him?
questions.explanation: Saul was waiting in the city of Damascus for further instructions after his encounter with Jesus (Acts 9:8-9).

questions.text: Who does John the Baptist prepare the way for in Mark 1?
questions.explanation: John the Baptist prepares the way for Jesus Christ, as mentioned in Mark 1:1 and throughout the chapter.

questions.text: Where was John baptizing people at the start of Mark 1?
questions.explanation: John was baptizing in the Jordan River, as stated in Mark 1:4 and 1:5.

questions.text: What did people confess when they were baptized by John?
questions.explanation: People confessed their sins when baptized, as described in Mark 1:5.

questions.text: What was the significance of the voice from heaven in Mark 1?
options: Jesus is the Son of God
questions.explanation: The voice from heaven declared Jesus as God's Son, affirming His identity (Mark 1:11).

questions.text: What was Jesus doing in the wilderness after his baptism?
questions.explanation: Jesus was in the wilderness fasting and being tempted by Satan (Mark 1:12-13).

questions.text: What did Jesus say when he began his ministry in Mark 1?
options: Repent and believe the good news
questions.explanation: Jesus proclaimed, 'The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe the gospel' (Mark 1:15).

questions.text: What early activity did Jesus do after calling his disciples in Mark 1?
questions.explanation: Jesus drove out an unclean spirit from a man in the synagogue, signifying His authority (Mark 1:23-26).

questions.text: What does Mark 1 emphasize about Jesus' authority?
options: His miracles and power over spirits
questions.explanation: Mark highlights Jesus' authority through His miracles and His power to cast out spirits, illustrating His divine authority (Mark 1:21-28, 34).

questions.text: What is Jesus teaching about children in Mark 10?
options: They should be brought to Him
questions.explanation: In Mark 10:13-16, Jesus welcomes children and says the Kingdom belongs to such as them, emphasizing their importance.

questions.text: What does Jesus say about how to inherit the kingdom of God?
questions.explanation: Jesus states in Mark 10:15 that receiving the kingdom requires becoming like a child, emphasizing humility and trust.

questions.text: What did the rich man ask Jesus in Mark 10?
options: What should I do to inherit eternal life?
questions.explanation: In Mark 10:17, the man asks Jesus what to do to inherit eternal life, prompting Jesus to remind him of commandments.

questions.text: How does Jesus respond to the man who desires to follow Him but has many possessions?
options: Tell him to sell his possessions and follow
questions.explanation: Jesus instructs in Mark 10:21 that he should sell what he has and follow Jesus to be truly rich in heavenly treasures.

questions.text: What is Jesus' attitude toward those who are considered last in Mark 10?
options: They are to be left behind
They are the greatest in the kingdom
They are not part of the kingdom
questions.explanation: In Mark 10:31, Jesus says that many who are last now will be first, indicating God's value for humility and servanthood.

questions.text: Why do the disciples rebuke those bringing children to Jesus?
options: Because they thought children distract Jesus
Because Jesus should not be bothered
questions.explanation: In Mark 10:13, the disciples rebuke those bringing children, but Jesus affirms their importance and welcomes them.

questions.text: What warning does Jesus give about riches in Mark 10?
options: It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle
Riches are a blessing from God
questions.explanation: Jesus states in Mark 10:25 that it is easier for a camel to pass through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God, highlighting the challenge of wealth.

questions.text: What did Jesus instruct His disciples to do as He approached Jerusalem?
options: Find a colt for Him to ride
questions.explanation: Jesus told His disciples to find a colt that was tied, as described in verses 2-3, to fulfill prophecy and enter Jerusalem riding on it.

questions.text: What was the significance of the colt that Jesus chose to ride?
options: It was a sign of royalty
It was a symbol of peace
questions.explanation: Riding on a colt symbolized Jesus as the Prince of Peace, fulfilling messianic prophecy, as noted in verses 7-9.

questions.text: How did the crowd respond as Jesus entered Jerusalem?
questions.explanation: The crowd spread palm branches and shouted 'Hosanna,' welcoming Jesus as King, as described in verses 8-9.

questions.text: What did Jesus do when He entered the temple and saw its disorder?
options: He overturned tables and chased out buyers
questions.explanation: Jesus drove out those buying and selling in the temple, expressing indignation at turning God's house into a marketplace (verses 15-16).

questions.text: What was Jesus' response when questioned about His authority after cleansing the temple?
options: He asked them about John's baptism
He said He was the Son of God
questions.explanation: Jesus responded by asking about John’s baptism, which made the religious leaders hesitant to answer, showcasing His wisdom (verses 27-33).

questions.text: What did Jesus curse after observing a fig tree?
questions.explanation: Jesus cursed the fig tree because it was not bearing fruit, symbolizing spiritual fruitlessness, as described in verses 12-14.

questions.text: What lesson can be learned from Jesus’ rebuke of the fig tree?
questions.explanation: The fig tree serves as a symbol of spiritual fruitfulness; Jesus emphasizes the importance of genuine faith and righteousness (verses 20-22).

questions.text: What did Jesus teach about prayer and forgiveness in this chapter?
questions.explanation: Jesus teaches that prayer should be accompanied by forgiving others to ensure our own prayers are heard (verse 25).

questions.text: What did Jesus say the parable in Mark 12:1-9 illustrated?
options: How to build a great house
questions.explanation: Jesus' parable describes farmers who reject the servants sent by the landowner, symbolizing how people have historically rejected God's prophets (Mark 12:1-9).

questions.text: How did the religious leaders attempt to trap Jesus with the question about paying taxes?
options: By asking about respect for God
By asking whether to pay taxes to Caesar
By asking about the greatest commandment
questions.explanation: They asked Jesus whether it was lawful to pay taxes to Caesar, aiming to find a reason to accuse Him (Mark 12:13-14).

questions.text: What was Jesus' response when asked about paying taxes to Caesar?
options: Render to Caesar what is Caesar's
Give to God what is God's
questions.explanation: Jesus responded with the famous phrase, 'Render to Caesar the things that are Caesar's,' emphasizing the distinction between earthly and divine obligations (Mark 12:17).

questions.text: What did Jesus say is the greatest commandment in Mark 12:29-31?
options: Love the Lord your God with all your heart
questions.explanation: Jesus summarized the law with the commandments to love God wholeheartedly and love others as oneself (Mark 12:30-31).

questions.text: Why did the scribes commend Jesus after His answer about the greatest commandment?
options: Because He healed a sick person
questions.explanation: The scribes believed Jesus' summary of loving God and neighbor was a correct and authoritative answer, reflecting an understanding of true devotion (Mark 12:32-34).

questions.text: What did Jesus praise about the poor widow’s offering in Mark 12:41-44?
options: She gave out of her abundance
questions.explanation: Jesus admired the widow because she gave all she had to live on, showing genuine sacrificial giving (Mark 12:44).

questions.text: In Jesus’ teachings in Mark 12, what is prioritized over riches?
questions.explanation: Jesus points to genuine faith and sacrificial giving, like the widow’s offering, as true priorities over material wealth (Mark 12:43-44).

questions.text: What is Jesus warning about in Mark 12:38-40 regarding the scribes?
questions.explanation: Jesus warns against the scribes' desire for honor and recognition, which often leads to hypocrisy and greed (Mark 12:40).

questions.text: In Mark 13, what sign does Jesus mention will be a cause for alarm among those in Judea?
questions.explanation: Jesus mentions that 'wars and rumors of wars' will be signs indicating troubling times (Mark 13:7).

questions.text: What does Jesus say about the ending of the present age in Mark 13?
options: It will happen suddenly and without warning
It will be gradual and predictable
It will be marked by widespread peace
It will end with a visible sky phenomenon
questions.explanation: Jesus warns that the end times will come like a thief in the night, unexpectedly (Mark 13:32-33).

questions.text: According to Jesus in Mark 13, what should believers do in the midst of persecution and trouble?
options: Look to the heavens for signs
questions.explanation: Jesus advises his followers to stay alert and be watchful, especially during times of persecution (Mark 13:33-37).

questions.text: What does Jesus say about the return of the Son of Man in Mark 13?
options: It will be visible to all, from heaven
It will be a quiet event
questions.explanation: Jesus teaches that the coming of the Son of Man will be seen by everyone, appearing in the skies (Mark 13:26-27).

questions.text: Which metaphor does Jesus use in Mark 13 to describe his followers' preparedness?
questions.explanation: Jesus compares his followers to servants who must stay alert and watch for their master’s return (Mark 13:34-37).

questions.text: What does Jesus say about the timing of the end in Mark 13?
options: It is known only to the Father
It will be in the spring
It will follow a seven-year tribulation
It will coincide with the Jewish festival
questions.explanation: Jesus explains that no one knows the day or hour except the Father (Mark 13:32).

questions.text: What does Jesus warn his disciples about in Mark 13 regarding false messiahs?
options: They will succeed in deceiving many
They will be ignored by the world
questions.explanation: Jesus warns that false messiahs and false prophets will deceive many people during the end times (Mark 13:22).

questions.text: How should believers respond to the signs and events described in Mark 13 according to Jesus?
questions.explanation: Jesus emphasizes the importance of staying alert, prepared, and faithful until his return (Mark 13:33-37).

questions.text: Which city was Jesus celebrating Passover in during Mark 14?
questions.explanation: Jesus was in Jerusalem for Passover, as highlighted in Mark 14:1-2.

questions.text: Who was the woman who anointed Jesus at Bethany?
questions.explanation: In Mark 14:3, a woman named Mary anoints Jesus with perfume in Bethany.

questions.text: What was the reaction of some guests to the woman's act of anointing Jesus?
questions.explanation: Mark 14:4-5 shows some guests criticized the woman for wasting perfume, claiming it could be sold and money given to the poor.

questions.text: What did Jesus say about the woman's act of anointing him?
options: She was preparing him for burial
questions.explanation: In Mark 14:8, Jesus commends the woman, saying she anointed him for burial.

questions.text: What did Jesus predict about his disciples during the Passover meal?
questions.explanation: Mark 14:18-21 records Jesus predicting that one of the disciples would betray him.

questions.text: Who identified Jesus to the soldiers in the garden?
questions.explanation: Judas identified Jesus to the soldiers by betraying him with a kiss, as described in Mark 14:44-45.

questions.text: What was Peter doing when Jesus was arrested?
questions.explanation: In Mark 14:54, Peter follows Jesus from a distance but stays nearby.

questions.text: What was Peter's response after denying Jesus for the third time?
questions.explanation: In Mark 14:66-72, Peter weeps bitterly after realizing what he has done when he denies Jesus three times.

questions.text: Who was present at Jesus' crucifixion according to Mark 15?
questions.explanation: Mary Magdalene was among those watching Jesus' crucifixion, as mentioned in Mark 15:40-41.

questions.text: What did the soldiers do after Jesus was crucified?
questions.explanation: The soldiers divided Jesus' garments and cast lots for His clothing (Mark 15:24).

questions.text: What was written above Jesus on the cross?
questions.explanation: The inscription was 'The King of the Jews,' as stated in Mark 15:26.

questions.text: How did some individuals respond to Jesus' death?
questions.explanation: Some, including a centurion, recognized Jesus' significance and expressed reverence (Mark 15:39).

questions.text: What happened at the moment Jesus died?
questions.explanation: The temple veil was torn in two from top to bottom at Jesus' death (Mark 15:38).

questions.text: What did the centurion say after Jesus' death?
options: Truly, this man was the Son of God
questions.explanation: The centurion declared Jesus to be the Son of God after witnessing His death (Mark 15:39).

questions.text: What was done to Jesus' body after His death?
options: Placed in a tomb by Joseph of Arimathea
questions.explanation: Joseph of Arimathea requested Jesus' body and placed it in a tomb (Mark 15:42-46).

questions.text: What did the women do after Jesus’ death?
questions.explanation: The women observed where Jesus was laid in the tomb, preparing to anoint Him later (Mark 15:40-47).

questions.text: Who is mentioned as risen in Mark 16?
